speaker: interviewer
text: August 22, 1995. This is tape 3. Now, Mr. Zelikson, you were talking to us about your arrival at Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaiserwald, that's correct. When I arrived there at Kaiserwald-- excuse me-- they took us, and they told us we have to work, make streets, take the farm product for the farmers. And we were there about beginning in 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It must've been by March or April-- April [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived? Or that's when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. 1941-- the end of '41, they send us to Kaiserwald. 1942-- because we were already there about four months in Kaiserwald-- they sent us to another concentration camp. That was in Germany-- Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they transport you? Were they just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trucks, trucks or a, a, a freight-- a freight train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that selection process?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us with trucks to a freight train. And with the freight train, they took us right to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that-- at least three or four days-- at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you arrived at Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in Stutthof. That was also the Jewish kapos. And we had to do all the hard labor. We had to cut stones. We had to make parts. And we had to do for the German to clean the trucks, clean the tanks, and do all the hard labor. In Stutthof, we were about, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of barrack were you in? Who was in your barracks with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All, all Jewish and a lot of Gypsies-- a lot of Gypsies. They arrested a lot of Gypsies. The women were separate, and the men were separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there all age groups in your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. There was-- of course, the little children must-- they had in a different compound. But-- like my age, they put us with all of the people because all that was capable to do work, they kept us in one barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were around 12, 13 years old now, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was 13, yeah. But I was strong. I was built strong. So-- they, they knew what were they were doing, the Germans. Because once they saw somebody weakling, either they shot him or they put him in a different place. But everybody had to work. And they used to give us soup. It was fish soup. And that was just to survive. And they gave us a raw potato. And if we did a lot of work and we were good, they gave us a half a piece of bread. That piece was that big, maybe that wide. And that's it, so we could have enough strength to work more. And then they transferred us to Neuengamme.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stutthof, about six months, about six months. In Neuengamme, they transferred us was in November, I think. I'm pretty sure because it started to get cold. They took us in Neuengamme. And we had to do a, a lot of work there. And we were working until about around 7 o'clock at night, early in the morning from maybe 6 o'clock in the morning until 7:00 at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And 8 o'clock, they picked up 12 children. And they said, come with us. We went with them. And they say-- and they give us big shears-- say, you see these women? Cut their hair. Were they women who were other inmates? Who were the women?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- the women were separate. We had to cut all the women their hair. So we cut the women their hair. And then we saw little children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was two ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, before you-- when you were cutting the hair, where were you doing this? Were you going into the women's barracks? Were you outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They brought them-- they brought them over there. They took-- they took us-- we were 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came over to a barrack. There was a lot of women. And they told us to take the shears and cut their hair. We cut the hair and put it in a basket, in a big, big basket. We cut their hair. And then we saw a lot of children. And the German says, they going in, in the-- in these barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we took all the children-- they went in the barracks. And we looked in there, about 20 minutes or so. They were all dead. I don't know what they did-- they gassed them or what. And they were all dead. And then the German says-- they'd give us a hatchet-- and cut the limbs. We cut the limbs. We cut the arm. And they said to put it in another basket.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the gas chamber? Did you go into a gas chamber to retrieve these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Till today, I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you that you saw these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a big barracks, had no windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just dead children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just dead-- they walked in. And when we took them out, they were all dead. And they took-- they-- we had to cut the limbs from them. And the, the rest-- there was two ovens. And we'd throw the bodies right into the ovens-- the children. We worked from 8 o'clock at night to about around 3 o'clock in the morning. And they told us if we ever say anything what we're doing, they're going to kill us, too. From then on, they kept us separate in a separate barracks. They didn't let us to go to the older people. They gave us better food. They fed us better. And every time we came, we had to make promises that we wouldn't say a word. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was quite a few hundred children. And then from Neuengamme they transferred us to Aurich. Aurich was not like a concentration camp, but like, for a Wehrmacht, the, German soldiers-- we should work for the German soldiers. We had to dig ditches, big ditches. That was already in 1944. Neuengamme we were there until '44, till end of '43. And '44, we went to Aurich. And we had to dig ditches so their tank-- when the tank will come, it stays in the ditch. Big ditches, we had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every time they were moving you, did you ever have a-- or know why you were being moved? Did they ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't ask, and they didn't tell us.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would just say, go get your things, now you're leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just-- take the clothes and go. Leave anything that we had. You know, sometimes you pick up some stuff which you find in the street. You had to leave everything. Just we had the stripe clothes because we didn't have our own clothes. It was-- I'll never forget it. It was black with white stripes, the hat they put us on. We had a big Jewish star-- I had on the armband Jewish star, big, yellow. And they transferred. They didn't-- they never told us where we going or how we going. They just did it. Then we went to Aurich, and we were making ditches. But then we heard that the-- what-- the German were bombed. We heard from people that Hamburg was bombed, Berlin was bombed. So we knew that maybe it was getting close. So from Aurich, they started to transfer us to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Aurich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aurich-- not too long, maybe two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were just doing hard work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard work, yeah. We finished the work. And they transferred us-- transferred us with the freight train. At that time we didn't eat for six days. They only gave us a little cup of water to survive. Food they didn't give us. A German took out a big loaf of bread. It must have been but a-- close to a three quarters of a foot and about four inches. And he, he was ready to start to eat the loaf of bread. But he had to relieve himself. So he walked away. And I saw it, and I grabbed the loaf of bread. I could have ate 20 loaf of breads. And we were 200 people in that freight train. And everybody looked at me. And I couldn't eat that loaf of bread. I took the loaf of bread-- I'll never forget it-- and I broke up in 200 pieces. I counted, and I gave everybody a piece of loaf of bread. My adopted brother is now in Canada. He was very sick. And he opened his eyes. And he looked at me. And he looked at the piece of bread. And I was so starved. I looked at his eyes, and I couldn't do it. I took my piece of loaf of-- from the loaf of bread, and I give it to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the concentration camp. We were in Stutthof, Neuengamme together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept getting transferred together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you always, like, sort of living together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close, yeah, close. Yeah, close. And then we came in Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen-- at that time, it was towards-- almost towards the end of 1944. It must've been [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] close to November, maybe towards the end of November. In Bergen-Belsen, we saw skeletons by the thousands. They weren't even buried. So the first thing-- I'll never forget his name-- Josef Kramer. He was the Nazi commandant, commander. And he told us to take shovels, dig graves, and put the skeletons, the bodies. It was-- excuse me for my expression-- it was stinking terrible, the odor from the bodies, because it was laying for maybe weeks or a month or so. And it was thousands-- women, men, all naked. And we had to bury them. And then we saw there was German on top of the post, old Germans. So I say to my friend, I says, you know something is wrong because I don't see the Nazis. All the Nazis-- we don't see all the German. And we, we start to get closer to the German. And he says, stop, I'll shoot. And when he started with the rifle, it clicked. But there was no bullets in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans sort of disappeared from sight?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis disappeared because the England was already on the way. And we saw they didn't have no bullets. So everybody start to jump. Of course, I was sick. I was very sick. And when the Englander moved in-- they came at night. They walked in. They put a cross on my forehead. And my friend also, they put a cross on his forehead. And they said, you're typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your friend who had been on the train you fed, that was your friend Martin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Martin's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kulbak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Martin had typhus. And now you had caught typhus as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all-- all in the freight train, we had typhus. So the Englander put a cross. And then they took us to a hospital. And I was there in the hospital, I don't know, maybe a month or so. And then they transferred us also in Bergen-Belsen. But the-- it was the Nazis that were watching us that were their living quarters, we took over their places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you're talking about after the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But while you were at Bergen-Belsen, that entire time, were you sick? Were you there-- were you working? Or were you just there because you were sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working, but by the-- by the end, I start to get sick. I didn't get sick right away, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was wrong? Did you know how sick you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was sweating. And my limbs were cramping up. And I couldn't eat. My mouth was dry. I couldn't drink. I wanted to drink, but I couldn't drink. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever take you to see a doctor of any kind in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They would kill you. We couldn't tell them that we were sick. If you did-- told the Nazis that you were sick, they would take the bayonet and stab you right there. We couldn't tell them anything. That was just maybe a couple of weeks before the England came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with Martin? You had said that on the train he had been very, very sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sick. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he recover or did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he recovered.

speaker: interviewer
text: He recovered on the train from the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we-- when he-- he came in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was better.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was able to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had-- he didn't have no food. And his body maybe couldn't take it. And they-- over there, they-- we had some food. And he got better, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when the Englander came in-- and there was chaos in the camp. And they said, we-- you've got to make-- we can't-- use our own soldiers because it's going to take up too much time. You've got to make your own police.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the commandant from the police was Vinneck-- his name. And he said, you look like a strong guy. You want to join the police? I said, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- you were getting cigarettes free, or getting chocolate free, and all kind of goodies. I said, sure. And I joined the police. That was in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I belonged to the Haganah. So I was off in the Haganah. One of the-- a Jewish man came. And he says, you want to go fight for Israel? At that time, Israel was fighting the Arabs. But in Bergen-Belsen now, the British had come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans had left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course. They ran away-- except they caught Josef Kramer. They caught him. He ran away. But he had a house maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two kilometers from the camp. He had a beautiful-- well, it was a Jewish house. He was living there and he thought the Englanders wouldn't find him. And they found-- they found him. They brought him back to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now the British were-- the Jewish people, all, and your friends were all staying in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: By choice? You just decided to stay on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They took it out from the old camp, from the concentration camp Bergen-Belsen, and they brought us where the German Nazis was living, their quarters. And we took their quarters. And that's where I became a policeman. And in 1946, they told us, everybody from the Haganah, we will volunteer to fight the Arabs to go to Israel. So I said, OK, we'll go to Israel. And we went with the ship. And there was about a little over 300 people. And we went to Israel. We were about 100 miles from Israel, a big, gigantic English ship-- then they told us it was a destroyer. They told us we could not go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Englander.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he on your boat or on the other boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They invaded our boat. They throw ropes. And they pulled us in. And they come on our boat, and they say we cannot go to Israel. The closest-- they could take us to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Haganah-- when they asked you from Bergen-Belsen if you wanted to go fight for Israel, did a lot of people go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- where did you meet? How did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to belong Betar. Betar--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar became Haganah. We used to have meetings. All the young people used to have, at night, meetings. And they used to tell us, you know, what goes on in Israel, and if we want to fight for Israel. And we told them, yes. We volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to the-- where was the boat? How did you get to the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Bremen. In Bremen, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the boat you were on?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], we called it-- [NON-ENGLISH]. As it was a German Merchant Marine boat, converted, you know, the-- from the German navy, small boat. So everybody called it [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that? You, you now had left and you were at Bremen. And a lot of you-- were you sneaking out onto the boat? Or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They, they, they, they took us with the big trucks-- we went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went from the camp-- of course, we sneaked out at night. But they took us with big trucks. And we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now the boat went. And you said that the, the British ship stopped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It stopped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And turned you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't let us go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they sent us to Cyprus. Cyprus-- we were there for, I don't know, three weeks, a month, or so. And they sent us back to Germany, sent us back to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they allow you to land at Cyprus and come off the boat and stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a, a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name? Or was there a place you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I have some books that-- from-- in Bergen-Belsen from the-- when they were writing the books, they had it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, I, I can't be too sure. It was a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sent us back to Bergen-Belsen. And then they said, who wants to volunteer to go to the United States? So of course, when we heard 'United States,' we volunteered.

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend, my adopted brother, he did some black-market dealings. And he got caught. And he couldn't go to the United States. So I went to the United States. And he went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who caught him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German police-- on the black market. Actually, not the German-- the Interpol. He was transporting coffee to Bielefeld. And they caught him with the truck, and they told him it's illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to the United States by--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to-- a ship of A Navy-- big, 60-ton Merchant-Marine ship. The name was And we came in in a storm. And fish kept on following the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the captain says, something is wrong. Somebody is dead. And he told the sailors to start to look. And they looked and they couldn't find. A woman, her husband died on the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she hid him under the bunk and put clothes and covered-- so they couldn't find it. And the captain says, somebody's dead. Because the-- when dolphins start to follow the ship, then there's a dead. And they finally found the person. And the woman started to scream, don't throw him in the water. But the, the law is that, once you die on the water, you have to go down underwater.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put him in a basket. They put an American flag. And everybody came. And they sang the International Anthem. And they let him go in the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came, HIAS brought me over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you-- you came to Ellis Island? Where did you come to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ellis Island, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you traveling alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I didn't have no-- I had friends. I had friends. But when I came into Ellis Island, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they says, we've got to give you some places to live. So they brought us on Broadway and 102nd Street. And they give us, like, a hotel. The HIAS was paying for it. And they were giving us $10 spending money. I was there about a month. And I told them, I don't want it. I don't want the money. I want a job. I want to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I was-- what, about 21, I think. No, no, less-- I'm sorry. 23, I got-- 25, I got married. So I had to be around 19, 19. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they say, they'll find me a job. And they told me there was a butcher place-- he's looking for somebody to learn the butcher. I told them, I know how to butcher because when I was a small kid, I helped my friend in the slaughterhouse to work on the butcher. So I started to work for the butcher. He was paying me $25 a week. They didn't let me go out to lunch.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- his wife gave me a cup of coffee with an old bagel that was hard as a rock. And she said, that's it, And would give me five minutes to finish it. After I finish, go to work. And I used to pluck chicken, cut the meat. And the women used to give me $0.10 for each chicken. And I thought I had the money. And when the women left, he says, no, the money belongs to me. You don't get nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a while, I told the women not to leave me any money because he's taking away. And they were very mad. So I worked for him about a month-- [? Markus ?] Market. [? Markus ?] was his name. And-- Where were you living then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living on 95th and Pitkin Avenue, near, near, near the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself or with others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no, by myself. And I told them, listen, I can't make a living. I can't have enough food. I need more money. He says, I'll give you $5. I said, no. I want $10 raise. He says, [GERMAN] so-and-so. And he got mad at me. He says, I can't afford to pay you no more. I was working--

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? Let's continue this on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
